reasoning:
Simple average 1.0679, weighted average 1.0617.

This is the critical interval for reported claim count development. After reviewing the triangle data from 2001–2024, reported counts jump sharply from age 11 (inception) to age 23 (12 months later). The averages show consistent growth across all periods. I selected 1.0675 as a midpoint between weighted (1.0617) and simple (1.0679) because both methods are roughly equally credible here. The 3-year averages are higher at 1.133, but those reflect more recent/volatile years; the 10-year averages (1.0962–1.1041) are more stable. The 6.75% factor appropriately captures the typical first-year reported count buildup for this book without over-weighting recent volatility.

Note: There is a modest CV across the period (0.013–0.0384), indicating some inter-year variation in the pace of early reporting. The selected LDF sits conservatively in the middle of that range.

Simple average 1.0037, weighted average 1.0033, all 3yr/5yr/10yr at 1.0000.

Reported counts show virtually no incremental development between ages 23 and 35. The triangle data is remarkably clean here—all historical year-pairs show near-zero growth. I selected 1.001 as a minimal tail factor to allow for negligible administrative processing or late-filed claims, but this interval is essentially mature by age 23. The consistency across every sub-cohort (3yr, 5yr, 10yr) all near 1.0000 is strong evidence that development is complete. No notable outliers or trends observed.

No data quality flags for this interval.

All averages: 1.0000–1.0005, CV near zero across all periods.

Reported counts are fully developed by age 35. The triangle shows absolute stability from age 35 onwards across all 24 accident years. I selected 1.0 as the tail LDF because any incremental development is immaterial (well under 0.1% in the averages). This is the first of many tail intervals where development is simply zero.

No data quality concerns.

All averages: 0.9998–1.0, CV near zero.

Continued stability with no meaningful development. The data shows a flat line; any slight variation (e.g., simple_all at 0.9999) is statistical noise. Tail factors are 1.0.

No data quality concerns.

Flat development continues. The reported count triangle is completely stabilized at this point. Tail LDF is 1.0.
